retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Buccal/Lingual Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Reducing the buccal and lingual surfaces is the third step in the preparation. The reduction of the buccal and lingual mucosa is optional. Some authors suggested preparing the buccal and lingual walls to create a gingivally inclined long bevel to make crown placement easier. On the other hand, others advocated for none to minimal (0.5 to 1 mm) preparation on the vestibular and lingual aspect of the crown, unless pronounced enamel convexity should only be reduced by a small amount. [12] Duggal and Curzon recommended trying the selected crown before lingual and buccal reduction. [13] Lastly, the operator should round all line and point angles. Occlusal-lingual and occlusal-buccal angles are beveled at 45 degrees. Bur is held parallel to the tooth’s long axis, and one should carefully blend the surfaces. Remove any remaining caries and perform appropriate pulp therapy if pulp involvement is present. The rubber dam should be left in place until cementing the crown. If it interferes with the seating and adaptation of the crown, it can be removed after tooth preparation. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional Methods for Anterior Access Cavity Creation
retrieved_docs.content: A fissure bur shapes the cavity outline incisally in a funnel or fan configuration. [54] [57] [58] Penetration into the pulp chamber proceeds with a surgical-length No. 2 or No. 4 round bur mounted on a slow-speed contra-angle handpiece. In cases of extensive pulpal recession, a No. 2 bur provides better control. Extension toward the incisal allows the bur shaft to operate nearly parallel to the long axis of the tooth. [38] [59] [57] Following penetration, the remaining tooth structure is removed with a round bur, including dentin beneath the lip of the chamber roof. [38] [59] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional Methods for Anterior Access Cavity Creation
retrieved_docs.content: The pulp cavity of anterior teeth is accessed through the exact center of the lingual surface with the traditional method for creating anterior access cavities. [52] Initial entry is performed with a round bur or a round-point tapering fissure bur held at a right angle to the tooth's long axis. [53] A contra-angle handpiece operating at an accelerated speed with water coolant guides this initial penetration. [38] [54] The preparation then extends conveniently toward the incisal, maintaining the bur's point in the central cavity and aligning the handpiece with the tooth's long axis. [55] High-speed instruments should not be used inside the pulp chamber, as they lack the tactile sensitivity required for controlled preparation. [38] [56] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Dentinogenesis imperfecta, especially DI type II, presents with greater pulpal tissue, which may complicate restorative interventions due to the higher frequency of pulpal exposure during restorative treatment or as a result of parafunctional habits.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional technique complications
retrieved_docs.content: Craniocervical perforation may develop when the convenience extension is left unfinished before the bur shaft enters the canal space. [63] [61] [62] Pulp debris left behind during preparation may also discolor the crown, especially when the access cavity lacks proper incisal extension and lies too close to the gingival margin. [63] [62] [40] Misjudgment of the tooth's lingual-axial or mesial-axial inclination can result in abrasion of the labial or distal wall during instrumentation. [63] [61] [62] Failure to complete the convenience extension can create a ledge at the apical-labial curve, where the instrument shaft rests on the shoulder or cavity edge instead of advancing smoothly into the canal. [63] [61] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth that have undergone endodontic access display more unrestorable fracture patterns compared to those that have not been accessed. [9] Beyond restorative etiological considerations, developmental and morphological factors may also contribute to a tooth's fracture risk. Occlusal anatomy characterized by deep fissures and steep cusp angles, potentially leading to altered intercuspation, pulp chamber volume changes, rotations, and inclinations, can further influence fracture risk. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Access Cavity Basic Principles
retrieved_docs.content: A preoperative radiograph offers critical details regarding the shape, size, number, and curvature of root canals. [46] Aligning the bur with the radiograph enables the estimation of the required depth of preparation, thereby reducing the risk of overextension or procedural errors. [47] [46] Thorough excavation of carious tissue and debris must follow, as residual material can promote reinfection within the canal. [47] [48] [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional Methods for Anterior Access Cavity Creation
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, "adult" teeth exhibit ovoid pulp chambers partially filled with secondary dentin. To enable the bur shaft and instruments to function in the central axis, convenience extension must be advanced further initially when the radiograph shows advanced pulpal recession. The preparation funnels down toward the canal orifice, which becomes increasingly difficult to reach with a round bur as the pulp recedes, often necessitating a deeper initial extension. [56] [59] [57] Such an extension ensures that the bur shaft and instruments remain aligned with the central axis, even in cases with a reduced pulp space. [56] [59] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Angle class I molar classification (also known as neutroclusion) is determined by the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar occluding with the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar. A class II molar classification (mesoclusion) is determined by the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar, occluding mesial to the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar. Lastly, a class III molar classification is determined by the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar occluding distal to the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar (distoclusion). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Indications for the use of stainless steel crowns: Following pulp therapy Multisurface caries restorations Patients at high risk of caries Deciduous teeth with developmental defects, such as amelogenesis imperfecta, dentinogenesis imperfecta, enamel hypoplasia Teeth with extensive wear Where it is likely that the restoration may fail, eg, the proximal box formed is extended beyond the anatomic line angles. Fractured teeth Abutment for space maintainer [6] Patients who are unlikely to attend follow up appointments [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional Methods for Anterior Access Cavity Creation
retrieved_docs.content: Once the canal orifice is located, the lingual shoulder is eliminated. This dentin shelf extends from the cingulum to approximately 2 mm apical to the orifice. A tapered safety-tip carbide or diamond bur removes this barrier. [38] [57] Proper technique requires placing the fine safety-tip diamond bur about 2 mm apical to the canal orifice and inclining it toward the lingual to slope the shoulder, while avoiding beveling of the incisal edge. Removal of the lingual shoulder produces a continuous, fluid preparation. [38] [54] [56] [59]

retrieved_docs.content: The 2 most common dental abnormalities are dental caries and malocclusion. Dental Caries

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The TrussAC is recommended when maintaining structural integrity is paramount, yet entry into multiple canals is required. This approach is most applicable in situations where canal orientations allow separate, small entry points and where restorative longevity and functional durability are key considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Static Guided Access Cavity
retrieved_docs.content: Dynamic changes (eg, changes in the bur's angulation, size, depth, or type) are not possible during treatment once the template has been created. [80] [84] [86]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Dens evaginatus/talon cusps have a good prognosis with appropriate management. When less severe, patients may be unaware of their presence, while more severe cases may warrant more invasive intervention. As long as proper restorative or endodontic protocol is followed, teeth that present with dens evaginatus have a good prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Static Guided Access Cavity
retrieved_docs.content: Alignment may be compromised by CBCT artifacts from extremely radiopaque restorations in teeth that require full-coverage restorations. [80] [83] [84]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 961
retrieved_docs.content: labor and delivery. Recognizing these interrelated conditions enables clinicians to anticipate potential delivery challenges, plan appropriate interventions (such as external cephalic version, cervical assessment, or operative delivery), and optimize outcomes for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2432
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester, is mapped to short cervical length in pregnancy, indicating that the condition of cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is understood as a short cervical length—a key obstetric measurement used to evaluate the risk of preterm birth and guide patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery of vertex presentation refers to a vaginal delivery of the fetus when the baby is positioned head‑first, representing a specific type of vaginal delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, second trimester, is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change occurring in the second trimester is considered a sign of early or threatened labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: cephalopelvic disproportion, cervical pathology, multiple gestation, and drug contraindications—provides a comprehensive framework for obstetric risk assessment, clinical decision‑making, and optimal maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is a fetal procedure that is classified among other delivery procedures, indicating that it is a type of intervention performed on the fetus to reposition it prior to birth and is grouped within the broader category of delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2038
retrieved_docs.content: Previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth is recognized as a specific manifestation of a previous c-section, indicating that a prior cesarean delivery can create complications in subsequent pregnancy or childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are recognized as a form of failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض) and are also classified as causes of a prolonged first stage of labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض) as well as a prolonged second stage of labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض). These classifications reflect that abnormal fetal positioning, size mismatch, or other intrapartum problems can impede the initiation of effective uterine contractions and can extend both early and later phases of the birthing process, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and appropriate obstetric interventions to prevent labor dystocia and improve maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2769
retrieved_docs.content: Low transverse cesarean section is clinically associated with تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes), indicating that this surgical approach to delivery may be linked to an increased occurrence of early membrane rupture, a factor that clinicians must consider when planning obstetric care and managing potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: assessment, early identification of abnormal presentations, cervical pathology, uterine functional disturbances, and multiple‑gestation status, together with avoidance of teratogenic drugs, are essential for individualized management strategies that optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 850
retrieved_docs.content: care during pregnancy, labor, and delivery. This integrated framework supports comprehensive obstetric assessment by recognizing how fetal presentation anomalies and maternal surgical history jointly inform the need for specialized care.

retrieved_docs.content: Nasogastric tube procedures classify the nasogastric tube, indicating that the range of clinical actions, techniques, and protocols are organized around the use, insertion, management, and care of the nasogastric tube.
